title: Parker House rolls
subtitle: Folded American butter rolls
excerpt: Twelve enriched American rolls folded in half over a smear of butter before their second prove, so the crease is buttery and tender and the roll springs open when pulled. Light, slightly sweet crumb, glossy top. Best eaten warm the day they are baked.
sourceNotes: The Parker House roll is named after the Parker House Hotel in Boston, Massachusetts, where it was served from the 1850s. The recipe appears in American domestic cookbooks from the late nineteenth century, many available through the Internet Archive, which document the folded, butter-creased roll as a standard hotel bread. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: folded enriched rolls with a butter-creased interior are documented in the chapter on dinner rolls, noting that the fold allows the roll to be split without cutting and produces a softer interior surface than a fully closed roll.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely and freeze in a bag. Refresh from frozen in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and fold the rolls, arrange on the tray, cover and refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Brush with butter for the last 3 minutes for a glossy, golden top

glossaryTerms.definition: The shaping technique that gives Parker House rolls their distinctive look: each oval of dough is brushed with butter, then folded in half so the two halves are slightly offset — the back half sits a little lower than the front. During proving and baking, the fold seals but remains soft, and the roll springs open when pulled, revealing the buttered crease inside.

content.text: Parker House rolls are defined by the fold: each roll is an oval of enriched dough brushed with butter and folded in half, with the top half sitting slightly back from the lower. The fold seals during baking but the crease stays soft and buttery, and the roll opens easily when pulled apart at the table. The crumb is fine-textured and slightly sweet.

items.prepNote: melted, for brushing the folds and the tops

content.text: Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} with the {{whole-milk}} and {{water}} for the dough and pour into the flour. Mix to a rough dough. Knead for 5 minutes, then add the {{unsalted-butter}} for the dough in three additions, working each piece in before adding the next. Knead for a further 5 minutes until smooth, soft, and elastic.
Cover and leave in a warm place for 60 minutes until doubled.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces (about 80 g each). Roll each piece into an oval roughly 10 cm long. Brush with the melted {{unsalted-butter}} for finishing. Fold each oval in half across the width, placing the top half slightly back from the lower so the fold is visible. This is the
. Press the fold lightly to hold, then arrange on the tray with 3 cm between rolls.
Cover loosely and leave for 40 minutes until puffed. Preheat the oven to 190°C fan.

Bake for 15 minutes, then brush the tops with the remaining melted butter and continue for 3–5 minutes until the tops are deep gold and the rolls feel set when pressed. Internal temperature should reach 93°C. Serve warm.
